drugs.notes: Give with food. Initial dose 0.2, maintenance 0.1 mg/kg
Single dose preferred in cats. Risk of renal toxicity with repeated dosing
Can be given as 4.4 mg/kg once daily or 2.2 mg/kg twice daily
COX-2 selective. Monitor hepatic function
Broad spectrum. Can combine with clavulanic acid
Same dosing as canine
Enhanced spectrum vs beta-lactamase producers
Same dosing as canine
Effective against anaerobes and Giardia. Neurotoxicity at high doses
Shorter duration in cats. Bitter taste, consider compounding
Fluoroquinolone. Avoid in growing dogs (cartilage damage)
Max 5 mg/kg in cats. Higher doses cause retinal degeneration
Give with food to reduce GI upset. First-line for tick-borne diseases
Effective for Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Bartonella
First-gen cephalosporin. Good for skin and soft tissue infections
Same dosing as canine
Anti-inflammatory: 0.5-1 mg/kg. Immunosuppressive: 1-2 mg/kg. Taper gradually
Use prednisolone NOT prednisone in cats (poor hepatic conversion)
Emergency anti-inflammatory. 7x potency of prednisolone
Broad-spectrum anthelmintic. Safe wide margin
Same dosing as canine
Heartworm prevention. AVOID in MDR1 mutant breeds (Collies)
Single dose for cestodes. Can repeat in 2-3 weeks
Single dose for cestodes
Proton pump inhibitor. Give 30 min before meals
Antiemetic (NK1 antagonist). Also has visceral analgesic properties
Same dosing as canine
Prokinetic + antiemetic. Avoid with GI obstruction
ACE inhibitor for CHF. Monitor renal values
Positive inotrope + vasodilator. Give 1h before meals
Loop diuretic for CHF/pulmonary edema. Monitor electrolytes
Opioid analgesic. Less effective than previously thought in dogs
Neuropathic pain. Also useful as anxiolytic pre-visit
Common pre-visit anxiolytic at 50-100mg per cat
Antihistamine. Causes sedation. Limited efficacy for atopy
Second-gen antihistamine. Less sedating
Pulse therapy for dermatophytosis: 1 week on, 1 week off
Same protocol as canine. Monitor hepatic function

interactions.effect: Severe GI ulceration, renal failure, coagulopathy
interactions.management: Never combine NSAIDs. Wait 5-7 days washout between switching
Never combine NSAIDs. Wait 5-7 days washout
Never combine NSAIDs. Wait 3-5 days washout

interactions.effect: High risk of GI ulceration and hemorrhage
interactions.management: Avoid concurrent use. If necessary, add GI protectant (omeprazole)

interactions.effect: High risk of GI ulceration and perforation
interactions.management: Avoid concurrent use. Taper corticosteroid before starting NSAID

interactions.effect: Increased risk of acute kidney injury
interactions.management: Monitor renal values. Ensure adequate hydration

interactions.effect: Reduced diuretic efficacy, risk of renal injury
interactions.management: Monitor urine output and renal values closely
Reduce doses of both when combining. Monitor closely

interactions.effect: Increased risk of seizures and neurologic signs
interactions.management: Use lower doses of metronidazole when combining
Common combination in CHF but requires careful dose titration and renal monitoring

interactions.effect: Ivermectin toxicity: ataxia, mydriasis, tremors, coma
interactions.management: Avoid combination especially in MDR1-mutant breeds
Monitor cardiac rhythm in predisposed patients
Choose one antiemetic based on cause of vomiting
